content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 11
Chapter 14 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 11 Part 3
14.3 Define consumerism and environmentalism, and explain how these concepts are linked to marketing strategies.

Part 3 - Consumer actions to promote sustainable marketing

Sustainable marketing (1 of 2)
Sustainable marketing is socially and environmentally responsible marketing that meets the present needs of consumers and businesses while also preserving or enhancing the ability of future generations to meet their needs.
The sustainable marketing concept considers both:
the societal marketing concept (i.e. the future welfare of consumers), and
the strategic planning concept (i.e. the company’s future needs).
Q: Can you think of some companies that practise sustainable marketing?

Figure 14.1
Sustainable marketing

Consumer actions to promote sustainable marketing (1 of 7)
Consumerism
Consumerism is an organised movement of citizens and government agencies whose aim is to improve the rights and power of buyers in relation to sellers.
Traditional buyer’ rights include the right (with some provisos):
not to buy a product offered for sale
to expect the product to be safe
to expect the product to perform as claimed.

Consumer actions to promote sustainable marketing (2 of 7)
Consumerism (cont.)
Consumer advocates call for additional consumer rights, such as the right to:
be well-informed about important aspects of the product
be protected against questionable products and marketing practices.
Q: Give examples of other advocated consumer rights. Do you think consumer rights go too far – or not far enough?

Consumer actions to promote sustainable marketing (3 of 7)
Environmentalism
Environmentalism is an organised movement of concerned citizens and government agencies that aims to protect and improve people’s current and future living environment.
Over the past several decades, environmental concerns have resulted in laws and regulations governing industrial commercial practices that affect the environment.
Q: Do you normally consider a producer’s environmental practices when you are making purchase decisions?

Consumer actions to promote sustainable marketing (4 of 7)
Environmentalism (cont.)
Environmental sustainability is a management approach that involves developing strategies that both sustain the environment and produce profits for the company.
Companies must consider:
‘greening’ activities (internal and external) that will pay off for the firm and the environment in the short run, and
‘beyond greening’ activities (internal and external) that will pay off in the longer term.
Q: Give some examples of ‘greening’ and ‘beyond greening’ activities.

Figure 14.2
The environmental sustainability portfolio
Source: Based on Stuart L Hart, ‘Sustainable value’, <www.stuartlhart.com/sustainablevalue.html>, October 2016.

Consumer actions to promote sustainable marketing (6 of 7)
Environmentalism (cont.)
Environmentalism creates some special challenges for global marketers.
Q: Can you think of some companies whose core mission is built around environmental sustainability? Does this contribute to or hinder their success?
What challenges does environmentalism create for global marketers?

Consumer actions to promote sustainable marketing (7 of 7)
Public actions to regulate marketing
Public concerns about marketing practices usually lead to public attention and legislative proposals.
The task is to translate legislation into the language that marketing executives understand as they make decisions about competitive relations, products, price, promotion and channels of distribution.
Q: Give some specific examples of issues faced by marketers in meeting their legal obligations when making decisions around (a) advertising, (b) distribution channels and (c) pricing.
